Article.ArticleTitle: Research of heat and mass transfer during the in-situ thermal desorption process of organic-contaminated soil: Sandbox experiment and dual-field modeling.

Abstract.AbstractText: In-situ thermal desorption (ISTD), while effective for soil remediation, faces critical sustainability challenges due to its high energy consumption and carbon footprint. These limitations stem primarily from insufficient understanding of the coupled multiphysics dynamics and pollutant migration behaviors governing process efficiency. To address this gap, this study integrated sandbox experiment with numerical modeling to investigate heat and mass transfer mechanisms during ISTD. Based on a scaled three-dimensional experimental system, we conducted a full-process ISTD experiment and constructed a dual-field model with coupled physical-pollutant fields. The soil temperature curves verified the synergistic effect of heat conduction and convection, with mean relative errors between simulated and measured temperatures not exceeding 7.34 % (R2 ≥ 0.977). The model effectively characterized the spatiotemporal evolution of temperature, moisture, and gas pressure fields, alongside simulating concentrations of five pollutants. Correlation analysis identified molecular weight, boiling point, and hydrophobicity as key parameters controlling remediation efficiency. While reasonably predictive for four pollutants (MRE: 27-38 %, NRMSE: 10-18 %), the model revealed limitations in simulating n-dodecane due to unmodeled separate non-aqueous phase formation. This underscores the necessity of incorporating three-phase dynamics for highly hydrophobic pollutants. These insights enable mechanism-driven ISTD optimization for improved energy efficiency and reduced environmental impact.
